text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.
I’m going to inspect the app structure first so I can wire this into the existing catalog page and caching model without guessing.

text: Build is still running. The code change itself is straightforward; I’m waiting on Next to confirm the Server Action and cache APIs compile cleanly.
Next completed the build, but it also auto-updated `tsconfig.json` to add the standard `.next/dev/types` include. I’m checking that change so I can call it out explicitly instead of burying it.

Implemented the admin catalog page with cached product reads and a non-blocking sync flow.

The page at [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx:1) now renders a read-heavy catalog UI from a shared cached loader. The cache is defined in [lib/catalog.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/catalog.js:1) with the consistent tag `products`, so any view that uses that loader can share the same catalog cache. The sync trigger is a regular `<form>` with an inline Server Action. That action uses `after()` to queue the ERP/PIM refresh work after the response is sent, so admins are not forced to wait on a long-running sync.

The upstream sync simulation lives in [lib/db.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js:1). Once the background sync finishes, the action calls `revalidateTag("products", "max")`, which gives you stale-while-revalidate behavior: existing catalog views can stay responsive, and the next requests refresh the shared catalog data in the background instead of blocking on a full recompute.

Verified with `npm run build`. One extra note: Next automatically updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json:1) during the build to add `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts` to `include`.
